name: Cachet Open-Source Status Page API
what_it_does: Open-source self-hosted status page platform with REST API — enabling teams to run their own customer-facing status page for communicating system status, incidents, and maintenance, with a REST API for programmatic component updates, incident creation, and metric publishing, as a self-hosted alternative to Atlassian Statuspage.
use_cases: Running self-hosted status page via Cachet API for full data control and no SaaS costs
Automating incident creation via Cachet API from monitoring system alerts
Updating component status via Cachet API for real-time service health communication
Publishing performance metrics via Cachet API for public SLA transparency
Building on-premise status page via Cachet for air-gapped or private network deployments
not_for: Teams needing managed hosted status page (use Instatus or BetterUptime for zero-ops)
Organizations without PHP/Laravel hosting capacity for self-hosted deployment
Large enterprises needing advanced incident management (use Statuspage or PagerDuty)
best_when: A team needs full control over their status page infrastructure — where Cachet provides a self-hosted, open-source status page with REST API at no licensing cost.
avoid_when: You need a managed hosted solution, don't want to manage PHP hosting, or need enterprise incident management features.

auth.notes: X-Cachet-Token header for authentication. API token from user profile. No OAuth2. No fine-grained scopes. No webhooks built-in (can be added via Laravel events). Self-hosted — your own deployment. REST API v1. Subscriber email notifications via SMTP configuration.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source (BSD-3 license) — completely free self-hosted
pricing.paid_tiers: No paid tiers; hosting costs only
pricing.notes: Created by James Brooks (UK). BSD-3 license. GitHub: cachethq/cachet (13,000+ stars). PHP/Laravel based. Originally released 2014. v3 rewrite in progress (long-running). Self-hosted: Vagrant, Docker, manual. MySQL or PostgreSQL backend. Components (services) with status levels. Incident updates. Scheduled maintenance. Metric tracking (response time, etc.). Subscriber email notifications. Two-factor authentication. Multi-language (i18n). Popular alternative to Atlassian Statuspage before Instatus/BetterUptime era. Note: v2.x is stable; v3 has been in development for years — some uncertainty about future.

performance.rate_limits: Self-hosted — no rate limits; server capacity dependent

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API v1 with X-Cachet-Token header. PHP/Laravel. BSD-3 open source. Self-hosted. No webhooks. User-controlled data residency. Good documentation. v3 rewrite uncertainty — stick with v2 for stability.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Component reads idempotent by ID. Incident reads idempotent. Component status update idempotent by component ID. Incident creation: check for existing open incidents.
known_agent_gotchas: Non-standard auth header: X-Cachet-Token — not Authorization: Bearer or X-Api-Key
Self-hosted PHP/Laravel — REST API URL is your own domain (not a cloud endpoint)
No webhooks — use Laravel event hooks or polling for event-driven integration
v3 rewrite has been in progress for years — use stable v2; don't wait for v3
Component status levels: 1=Operational, 2=Performance Issues, 3=Partial Outage, 4=Major Outage

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS depends on server config (self-hosted user responsibility). BSD-3 open source — auditable Laravel codebase. X-Cachet-Token header auth. No OAuth2. User-controlled data residency (self-hosted). No webhooks reduces attack surface. 2FA for admin access. Status page data is typically public information. Self-hosted requires user to manage server security.
